title: A Tour of Frankenstein's Sets: Production Designer Tamara Deverell Interview
text: Production designer Tamara Deverell guided journalist Anthony Breznican through the elaborate sets of Guillermo del Toro's Frankenstein, showcasing the meticulous craftsmanship behind the film's gothic world. The centerpiece is a basement chamber beneath an 18th-century water tower where Dr. Frankenstein chains his creature. Deverell designed the space to evoke anatomical imagery, explaining that it features this skeletal feel with stone arches resembling a ribcage. The flooded canal running through the center represents two chambers of a heart, dividing the space while serving the narrative of creation and confinement. A massive Medusa sculpture dominates the laboratory, serving multiple thematic purposes. Originally designed as a water spout for the tower's cistern, it now looms over Victor's assembly of corpses. The artist notes that the myth's self-reflection element mirrors Victor's moment of horrified realization. The voltaic batteries—tall structures of green glass—shift to red when powered, symbolizing the transformation from life-giving energy to death and destruction. The construction involved: 3,178 workdays built the sailing ship, 2,830 workdays constructed the laboratory, and 1,200 spent on the exterior tower top. The production utilized 119 sets, with 24 built entirely on soundstages.
